fact: The Sonic Classifier is a lightweight pre-generation classifier inside ChatGPT Search that determines whether a user prompt requires fresh web retrieval before a response is generated.
context: The classifier fires before any response generation. It evaluates the prompt against a search-need threshold estimated at 65%. Prompts crossing the threshold trigger the fan-out engine; prompts below it use training data only.
application: Write prompts and page content to answer questions that would cross the 65% search threshold. Use time-sensitive framing, recency signals, and fact-based language to increase the probability your content is retrieved during search-triggered responses.

fact: The ChatGPT fan-out engine expands a single user prompt into multiple parallel search queries sent simultaneously to retrieval providers, with Standard mode firing 1-3 queries and Thinking/Deep Search mode firing 10-30+ recursive fan-outs.
context: Fan-out is the core mechanism by which AI search systems maximize retrieval coverage. Each fan-out query targets a different aspect, synonym cluster, or entity variant of the original prompt. Results from all queries are merged via RRF fusion before being fed to the generation model.
application: Map every content page to a cluster of 30-50 distinct fan-out queries an AI system might generate for your topic. Ensure each query variant is answerable somewhere in your content. Gaps in coverage represent citation risk.

fact: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) is the merge algorithm used to combine results from multiple fan-out query streams into a single ranked list before passing retrieved content to the generation model. RRF scores each document as the sum of 1/(rank+k) across all query streams where the document appeared.
context: RRF was introduced in academic literature and adapted for production search systems. In AI search pipelines, it allows results from semantic embedding search, BM25 keyword search, and structured data lookup to be merged into one coherent result set without requiring score normalization across systems.
application: To score highly in RRF merges, a content asset must appear in multiple query stream results, not just one. Build content that answers multiple related queries. Use entity anchoring and consistent terminology so the same page surfaces across synonym variants.

fact: AI search systems classify incoming prompts into a taxonomy of types including: informational, navigational, transactional, local/maps, shopping/product, news/recency, images/visual, and conversational/chat, and route each type to different retrieval providers and ranking signals.
context: The taxonomy determines which vertical pipeline is activated. Informational prompts use web index + embedding search. Shopping prompts route to Mercury/product graph. Maps prompts trigger local knowledge graph. News prompts apply recency filters. Images trigger visual search providers. Understanding which type a query resolves to predicts which content format and schema will be retrieved.
application: For each target query cluster, identify its prompt type. Create content assets in the format expected by that type: FAQ pages for informational, product pages with schema for shopping, entity pages with sameAs for navigational, and timestamped articles for news prompts.

fact: ChatGPT Search applies three recency filter windows: 7 days (breaking news), 30 days (general news and recent events), and 365 days (established topics where recent updates are needed). Content without machine-readable date signals is deprioritized in recency-sensitive query streams.
context: The training data cutoff means older content is already known to the model. Web search is invoked to fill the recency gap. The 7-day window applies to prompts classified as breaking news. The 30-day window applies to most current-events prompts. The 365-day window applies to topical queries where the model expects updated information.
application: Always include dateModified in JSON-LD structured data. Update key pages regularly. For time-sensitive topics, ensure your content publication or modification date falls within the relevant window. A page that was last modified 400 days ago will not surface in the 365-day recency window.

fact: ChatGPT Search uses SerpAPI (scrapes Google) for standard web retrieval, SearchAPI.io for Shopping results, and Labrador/Bright for image search. Bing powers direct web index access for Copilot. Perplexity uses Bing as its primary web provider.
context: Understanding the provider ecosystem allows content owners to optimize at the source: appearing in Google's index is sufficient for ChatGPT web retrieval, but appearing in Bing's index is required for Copilot. Shopping visibility requires Google Shopping and Bing Shopping catalog optimization. Image visibility requires proper alt-text, filenames, and image schema.
application: Ensure pages are indexed in both Google and Bing. Submit sitemaps to Bing Webmaster Tools separately. For shopping content, maintain clean Google Merchant Center and Bing Merchant Center feeds. For image content, use descriptive filenames and structured alt-text.

fact: ChatGPT's shopping results are powered by a pipeline codenamed Mercury, which retrieves product data via SearchAPI.io from Google Shopping. Products need valid Product schema markup, accurate pricing, and availability signals to appear in Mercury-powered shopping responses.
context: Mercury activates when a prompt is classified as a shopping or product-comparison query. The pipeline retrieves structured product data from Shopping feeds rather than generic web pages. Products without proper structured data, out-of-stock signals, or inconsistent pricing between the page and the feed are ranked lower or excluded.
application: Implement Product schema with price, availability, brand, sku, and image properties. Maintain an accurate Google Merchant Center feed. Ensure product prices on the page match the feed exactly. Use AggregateRating schema when reviews exist.

fact: AI systems use a combination of local knowledge graph data (sourced from Google Maps, Apple Maps, Yelp, TripAdvisor) and real-time web search to answer local queries. A business must have a verified Google Business Profile with complete NAP (name, address, phone) data to appear in AI-generated local answers.
context: Local queries trigger a different pipeline than general web retrieval. The AI system checks structured business entity records before web pages. Businesses with incomplete or inconsistent NAP data across directories are poorly disambiguated and frequently omitted from AI local answers.
application: Verify and optimize your Google Business Profile. Ensure NAP consistency across all local directories. Add LocalBusiness schema to your website with @id, name, address, telephone, openingHours, and geo properties. Submit to Apple Maps Connect. Build citations on Yelp, TripAdvisor, and industry-specific directories.

fact: News content must include NewsArticle schema with datePublished, dateModified, author, and publisher properties to be eligible for news-vertical retrieval in AI search systems. Without these signals, news pages are treated as generic web content and excluded from recency-filtered news pipelines.
context: AI systems maintain a separate pipeline for news-classified content. Pages that satisfy NewsArticle schema requirements, have a publication date within the relevant recency window, and are indexed in Google News or Bing News are eligible. Byline authorship and publisher identity signals increase retrieval probability in news mode.
application: Add NewsArticle schema to all time-sensitive articles. Include datePublished and dateModified in both the schema and the HTML meta tags. Register your site with Google News and Bing News publishers. Add author schema with sameAs linking to the author's Wikipedia or authoritative profile.

fact: AI image retrieval uses visual search providers (Labrador, Bright) that index images with associated metadata including alt-text, filename, surrounding text context, page URL, and ImageObject schema. Images without descriptive alt-text and structured metadata are ranked significantly lower in visual retrieval pipelines.
context: When a user prompt is classified as a visual query, the AI fan-out engine fires queries to visual search providers in addition to standard web providers. The relevance score for image retrieval combines visual similarity (via embedding), textual metadata relevance, and page authority signals.
application: Write descriptive alt-text for every image using subject-verb-object structure. Use descriptive image filenames (e.g., croutons-content-architecture-diagram.png not IMG_001.png). Add ImageObject schema with caption, description, and author. Ensure image pages load without JavaScript for AI crawler access.

fact: ChatGPT uses a proprietary Named Entity Recognition system with disambiguation format: entity["category","name","disambiguation_string"]. Categories include people, company, place, product, and event. The gpt-5-instant model generates entity sidebars. Entities not in the semantic knowledge graph are frequently omitted from AI-generated answers.
context: Entity disambiguation is critical for brands with common names, acronyms, or names shared with other entities. The disambiguation string helps the model distinguish between, for example, Apple (the company) and apple (the fruit). Entities well-represented in Wikipedia, Wikidata, and linked data sources are more reliably recognized and cited.
application: Create a Wikipedia page and Wikidata entry for your organization. Add sameAs properties in Organization schema linking to Wikipedia, Wikidata, Crunchbase, and LinkedIn. Ensure your brand name is used consistently across all web properties. Build a brand facts page with structured entity data.

fact: Retrieval surface engineering is the practice of distributing a single knowledge asset across multiple retrieval surfaces simultaneously, including: web pages, NDJSON streams, JSON-LD graphs, FAQ schema, HowTo schema, structured data feeds, and API endpoints, to maximize the probability of appearing in any one AI retrieval pipeline.
context: A single piece of content can be made retrievable via web search, structured data extraction, FAQ answer matching, product feed parsing, news pipeline indexing, image retrieval, and local knowledge graph lookups — each a separate retrieval surface. Appearing on only one surface creates a single point of failure in AI citation probability.
application: For each key content asset, build at least five parallel retrieval surfaces: the web page itself, FAQPage schema, HowTo schema (if applicable), an NDJSON export, and a JSON-LD graph. Distribute the same atomic facts across all surfaces using consistent entity naming.

fact: The Inference Cost Score measures how much computational work an AI model must perform to extract a useful fact from a content asset. It is scored across six dimensions (0-5 each, lower is better): atomicity, context completeness, structure, ambiguity, entity clarity, and freshness signaling.
context: High inference cost content contains ambiguous pronouns, vague references, missing dates, inconsistent entity names, and multi-claim sentences. AI models must resolve these ambiguities before using the content, increasing inference cost and decreasing retrieval probability. Low-cost content is atomic, explicit, and machine-parseable.
application: Score every key content asset using the six inference cost dimensions before publishing. Target a total score below 8 (out of 30). The RetrievalAdvantage metric is computed as 1 / (1 + InferenceCostScore). Content with a score of 8 has a RetrievalAdvantage of 0.11; content with a score of 2 has a RetrievalAdvantage of 0.33.

fact: Croutonization is the process of converting raw content into atomic, machine-parseable knowledge units called croutons, each containing exactly one verifiable claim (FACT), the necessary context to interpret the claim (CONTEXT), the operational use of the claim (APPLICATION), and a source URL (SOURCE).
context: Traditional content is written for human reading: flowing prose, complex sentences, multiple claims per paragraph. AI retrieval systems perform better on atomic, explicit, self-contained facts. Croutonization is the editorial process of decomposing existing content into the smallest retrievable unit that retains full meaning.
application: For each content page, identify all distinct factual claims and decompose them into individual croutons. Each crouton should pass the self-contained test: a reader with no prior context should be able to understand the crouton without reading the surrounding text. Eliminate pronouns without clear antecedents, relative references, and implied context.

fact: Entity anchoring is the practice of using consistent, fully-qualified entity names throughout a content asset to prevent AI systems from treating variants as different entities. The primary entity should be named identically in the title, meta description, H1, first paragraph, schema markup, and all crouton fact statements.
context: AI systems use entity co-occurrence patterns to build knowledge graphs. If a brand is referred to as 'Croutons Agents' in the title, 'the platform' in body text, and 'it' in the conclusion, the AI model cannot reliably anchor these references to the same entity. This reduces the probability of the entity being correctly cited.
application: Define canonical entity names for your brand, products, people, and locations. Use these canonical names consistently across all content assets. Never substitute pronouns for entity names in fact statements. Use sameAs links in schema to anchor your entities to external knowledge graph nodes.

fact: AI search citations exist on a spectrum from explicit (numbered inline citations [1][2][3] visible to users) to implicit (content that influenced the response but is never shown). Content with ref_type 'academia' (Wikipedia, arXiv) is used for grounding but never displayed to users. Explicit citations generate referral traffic; implicit citations do not.
context: ChatGPT distinguishes between source types in its internal citation system. On-demand retrieval sources receive explicit citations in the response. Academic and authoritative grounding sources receive hidden grounding treatment. The distinction matters for SEO value: explicit citations generate click-throughs; implicit grounding provides entity validation only.
application: Structure commercial content to earn explicit citations by writing in web-page format (not academic format) with clear, attributable claims. Use FAQ and HowTo schema to trigger structured answer inclusion. Academic authority content (Wikipedia, research papers) can be used as supplementary sameAs links to boost entity authority without expecting direct referral traffic.

fact: FanOutCoverage is defined as: covered_queries / total_queries, where covered_queries is the count of distinct fan-out query variants for which your content appears in the top 10 retrieval results, and total_queries is the total size of the fan-out cluster for that topic. A FanOutCoverage score of 1.0 means your content answers every query variant the AI might generate.
context: FanOutCoverage is the primary KPI for AI search optimization. Unlike traditional SEO which measures ranking for a fixed keyword list, AI search coverage must be measured against a probabilistic query cluster. The cluster is generated by predicting all query variants the fan-out engine might produce from a user's original prompt.
application: For each target topic, generate a fan-out cluster of 30-50 query variants. Audit which variants your content answers. Calculate FanOutCoverage = covered / total. Prioritize content additions based on uncovered queries. Target FanOutCoverage above 0.75 for primary topics.

fact: The composite retrieval_probability for a content asset is computed as: retrieval_probability = FanOutCoverage x RetrievalAdvantage x EntityAuthority x RetrievalSurfaceCount, where each factor is normalized to a 0-1 scale. This formula produces a single score representing the probability that a content asset will be retrieved and cited by an AI system for a given topic.
context: The four factors are multiplicative, not additive. A score of 0 on any single factor produces a composite score of 0. This means a page with perfect content but no entity authority, or perfect entity authority but poor fan-out coverage, will have a near-zero retrieval probability. All four dimensions must be optimized simultaneously.
application: Measure all four components for each key content asset. Identify the lowest-scoring component and prioritize improving it first, as it is the binding constraint on overall retrieval probability. A common failure pattern is high FanOutCoverage with low RetrievalSurfaceCount, which is easily fixed by adding schema and NDJSON exports.

fact: The optimal content architecture for AI retrieval is: Hero Answer (direct answer to the primary query, 40-60 words) > TLDR (3-5 bullet summary) > Crouton Blocks (atomic fact sections) > Explanation Layer > Operator Playbook > Measurement > FAQ > Entities > References > Machine Layer Output (NDJSON and JSON-LD).
context: This architecture is designed so that AI crawlers, which parse content top-to-bottom without executing JavaScript, encounter the most retrievable content first. The Hero Answer satisfies the 'answer first' rule for featured snippet and AI answer box eligibility. The Machine Layer Output at the bottom provides structured data for embedding-based retrieval systems.
application: Restructure existing content pages to match this architecture. The Hero Answer should directly answer the page's primary question in one paragraph. Each Crouton Block should contain one atomic fact with its context and application. The FAQ section should use FAQPage schema with questions derived from the fan-out query cluster.

fact: The optimal title format for AI-retrievable pages is: Primary Question | Secondary Context | Brand. Meta descriptions should be 140-155 characters, answer-first (state the answer in the first clause), include a secondary synonym of the primary keyword, and contain the brand name. Meta descriptions are displayed approximately one-third of the time by Google and are used directly by ChatGPT and Perplexity for content previewing.
context: Title tags serve as the primary retrieval signal in keyword-based search and as the entity label in semantic search. The pipe-separated format creates three distinct signals: the question (matches conversational queries), the context (matches navigational and informational queries), and the brand (establishes entity anchoring). Meta descriptions that start with the answer have higher click-through rates and higher AI citation probability.
application: Audit all page titles and meta descriptions against this format. Rewrite titles that use keyword-stuffed formats (Keyword 1 | Keyword 2 | Keyword 3) to use question-first format. Ensure every page has a unique meta description that begins with an answer to the page's primary question.

fact: The minimum enterprise schema stack for AI-retrievable pages consists of: WebPage (or subtype), BreadcrumbList, Organization (with sameAs links), Article or TechArticle (with dateModified), FAQPage, and HowTo (where applicable). All schema nodes must use stable @id URLs, and dateModified is required on every page to be eligible for recency-filtered pipelines.
context: Search engines and AI retrieval systems use schema markup as high-confidence structured signals that override ambiguous text signals. Pages without schema are treated as lower-confidence sources. The Organization schema with sameAs links is particularly critical for entity disambiguation. Without it, the AI system cannot reliably anchor the brand entity to a node in its knowledge graph.
application: Implement all six schema types on every key content page. Use a shared Organization schema object on all pages. Ensure @id values are consistent across pages. Validate schema using Google's Rich Results Test and Schema.org validator. Update dateModified on every meaningful content update.

fact: NDJSON (Newline-Delimited JSON) is the preferred machine-layer output format for crouton knowledge bases. Each line is a valid JSON object representing one crouton with fields: crouton_id, entity_primary, entities, fact, context, application, source_url, source_type, tags, and confidence. NDJSON files are parseable by embedding pipelines, vector databases, and structured data extraction systems.
context: NDJSON is used by major data processing systems (Apache Spark, Elasticsearch, MongoDB) as a streaming data format. AI embedding systems can ingest NDJSON files directly to populate vector stores. Serving NDJSON at a stable URL (e.g., /api/knowledge.ndjson) allows AI-powered indexing tools to consume your knowledge base without scraping your web pages.
application: Export your crouton knowledge base as a publicly-accessible NDJSON file. Link to it from your sitemap and robots.txt. Use the crouton schema consistently. Update the file when content changes. Include the NDJSON URL in your Organization schema's subjectOf property.

fact: A JSON-LD knowledge graph for AI retrieval should include a top-level @graph array containing interconnected nodes for the Organization, WebSite, WebPage, and all BreadcrumbList items, with each node connected via @id references. The graph enables AI systems to traverse relationships between entities rather than treating each page as an isolated document.
context: JSON-LD's @graph structure allows multiple schema types to be defined in a single script block with cross-references. This enables the AI system to understand, for example, that the Article on a page was authored by a Person who is a memberOf the Organization that owns the WebSite. These relationship chains are the raw material for knowledge graph construction.
application: Consolidate all page-level schema into a single JSON-LD @graph block. Use consistent @id values across pages (e.g., https://yourdomain.com/#organization for the Organization node). Validate the graph using the JSON-LD Playground. Ensure all entity nodes have sameAs links to external knowledge graph nodes.

fact: ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini AI crawlers do not execute JavaScript. Bing Copilot's crawler executes JavaScript with approximately 53ms timeout. Grok executes JavaScript with 1-2 second timeout. Content rendered via JavaScript (React, Vue, Angular SPAs) is invisible to non-JS crawlers unless a static HTML fallback is provided.
context: This is the single most important technical fact for AI visibility. An estimated 60-80% of modern web content is partially or fully JavaScript-rendered. AI crawlers that cannot execute JavaScript see an empty page or a loading spinner. The content in that page does not exist for AI retrieval purposes.
application: Add noscript tags containing the full page content as static HTML. Use server-side rendering (SSR) or static site generation (SSG) for all key content pages. Verify AI crawler visibility by disabling JavaScript in your browser and checking if the full content is still visible. Set up Cloudflare to serve static cached pages to known AI crawler user-agents.

fact: Embedding-based retrieval converts text into dense vector representations and finds semantically similar content using cosine similarity or approximate nearest neighbor search. AI search systems use embedding retrieval alongside keyword (BM25) retrieval, and RRF fusion merges the two result streams.
context: Embedding retrieval captures semantic similarity that keyword search misses. A document about 'affordable domain registration' may not match a keyword query for 'cheap web addresses' but will score highly in embedding similarity. This means content that covers a concept thoroughly — even without using exact keyword phrases — can still appear in fan-out retrieval streams.
application: Optimize content for conceptual completeness, not just keyword density. Cover the topic from multiple angles, using synonyms, related concepts, and entity variants naturally. Provide a clean, embedding-ingestible NDJSON export that allows AI systems to compute embeddings from your structured croutons rather than noisy HTML.

fact: The Hero Answer block is a 40-60 word direct answer to the page's primary question, positioned as the first visible content on the page after the title. A Hero Answer that directly answers the page's target query in the first paragraph increases eligibility for both Google featured snippets and AI answer box inclusions.
context: Featured snippets are extracted from the first content block that directly answers the query. AI systems similarly prefer content that provides a direct answer without requiring the model to infer the answer from surrounding context. The 40-60 word length is optimal because it is long enough to be complete but short enough to be extracted without truncation.
application: Write a Hero Answer for every key content page. The format is: [Entity] is/does/has [claim]. [Supporting detail in one sentence]. [Relevance or application in one sentence]. Test each Hero Answer for self-containment: it must make complete sense as a standalone extracted answer.

fact: FAQPage schema should contain 5-10 question-answer pairs derived from the page's fan-out query cluster. Each question must be phrased exactly as a user would ask it (conversational, not keyword-stuffed). Each answer must be self-contained (50-150 words) and include the entity name. FAQPage schema appears in Google's rich results and is used directly by AI systems for structured answer retrieval.
context: FAQ schema creates a structured retrieval surface that AI systems can query directly without parsing prose. The question-answer format maps directly to the conversational query format used in AI search. Including FAQ questions derived from the fan-out cluster ensures that each question in the schema matches a real query variant the AI might generate.
application: For each content page, generate 5-10 FAQ questions from the page's fan-out cluster. Write answers that begin with the answer (not a restatement of the question). Include the entity name in each answer. Validate with Google's Rich Results Test. Update FAQ questions quarterly based on new fan-out analysis.

fact: The Atomicity Rule states that each crouton fact statement must contain exactly one verifiable claim. A fact statement that contains two claims (e.g., 'X does A and Y does B') must be split into two separate croutons. Compound claims increase inference cost because the AI model must parse and verify each component independently.
context: Atomicity is the most important writing rule in the Croutonization spec. Compound claims are common in natural writing but create retrieval friction. When an AI system retrieves a compound claim, it must verify both components before using the content. If one component is incorrect or unverifiable, the entire crouton is discarded.
application: Review every fact statement in your content and test it with the atomicity rule: does it contain exactly one claim that can be independently verified? If not, split it. Common compound claim patterns to split: X does A and B; X is Y which means Z; X was created in year by person.

fact: The No-Pronoun Rule in Croutonization states that no crouton fact, context, or application statement may use a pronoun (it, they, this, that, their) without a fully-qualified antecedent in the same sentence. Every entity reference must use the canonical entity name.
context: Pronouns are the primary source of entity ambiguity in AI retrieval. When a crouton says 'it processes 1 million queries per day,' the AI model cannot determine which entity 'it' refers to without reading surrounding text. If the crouton is extracted from context (as it will be in retrieval), the pronoun becomes unresolvable and the crouton is either discarded or incorrectly attributed.
application: After writing any crouton, perform a pronoun audit: scan for it, its, they, their, this, that, these, those. Replace each with the canonical entity name. Example: 'it processes queries' becomes 'Croutons Agents processes queries per day.'

fact: Crouton fact statements must include specific numbers, dates, or quantitative ranges rather than vague quantifiers. Replace 'many users' with 'over 10,000 users'. Replace 'recently' with the specific date or quarter. Replace 'fast' with a specific metric (e.g., 'processes in under 200 milliseconds'). Vague quantifiers increase inference cost and reduce citation probability.
context: AI systems prefer quantitative, verifiable claims over qualitative ones. A claim that 'X is fast' cannot be verified and adds no information density. A claim that 'X processes requests in under 200 milliseconds on average' is precise, verifiable, and distinguishable from competitors. Temporal vagueness (recently, soon, in the past) prevents recency filter matching because the AI cannot determine if the claim falls within the relevant freshness window.
application: Audit all fact statements for vague quantifiers and temporal references. Replace each with a specific number, date, or range. If the specific number is not known, use a conservative range. Include the measurement context (e.g., 'in Q1 2025 testing on gpt-4o-mini').

fact: RetrievalAdvantage is computed as: 1 / (1 + InferenceCostScore), where InferenceCostScore is the sum of six 0-5 sub-scores (atomicity, context completeness, structure, ambiguity, entity clarity, freshness signaling). A perfect score of 0 produces a RetrievalAdvantage of 1.0; a score of 30 produces a RetrievalAdvantage of 0.032.
context: The RetrievalAdvantage formula is derived from the information retrieval concept of retrieval difficulty. Content that requires more inference work to process is less likely to be retrieved in latency-sensitive AI pipelines that must return results within milliseconds. The diminishing returns curve means that improving from a score of 20 to 10 produces much larger RetrievalAdvantage gains than improving from 10 to 5.
application: Calculate InferenceCostScore for each key page by scoring it on all six dimensions. Prioritize pages with scores above 15 for immediate croutonization. Use the RetrievalAdvantage value in your content quality dashboard. Target a RetrievalAdvantage above 0.25 (InferenceCostScore below 3) for top-priority content.

fact: Bing's web index is the primary retrieval provider for Microsoft Copilot and a secondary provider for Perplexity AI. Content indexed in Bing but not Google will appear in Copilot-powered responses. Separate Bing sitemap submission via Bing Webmaster Tools is required; Google Search Console submission does not automatically index pages in Bing.
context: Many SEO practitioners treat Google indexing as synonymous with web indexing. For AI search coverage across Copilot, this is incorrect. Bing's crawler (Bingbot) operates independently. Pages blocked in robots.txt for Bingbot will not appear in Copilot responses regardless of their Google ranking.
application: Create a Bing Webmaster Tools account and submit your sitemap. Verify Bingbot is not blocked in robots.txt. Check Bing's URL Inspection tool for indexing status. Monitor Bing-specific technical errors (crawl errors, indexation issues) that differ from Google's.

fact: EntityAuthority measures the degree to which an entity (brand, person, place, product) is represented in authoritative external knowledge sources including Wikipedia, Wikidata, Crunchbase, LinkedIn, and domain-specific databases. EntityAuthority is scored 0-1 based on the count and quality of external entity nodes with sameAs links to the entity.
context: AI systems assign higher confidence to entities that exist in multiple authoritative knowledge graph nodes. A brand with a Wikipedia page, a Wikidata entry, a Crunchbase profile, and proper Organization schema with sameAs links is a high-authority entity. A brand with only its own website is a low-authority entity and is more likely to be omitted or misattributed in AI-generated responses.
application: Build external entity presence systematically: Wikipedia page (requires notability), Wikidata entry (open contribution), Crunchbase profile, LinkedIn company page, and industry-specific databases. Add sameAs links in Organization schema pointing to each external node. Monitor entity recognition accuracy by testing your brand name in AI systems quarterly.

fact: A surface distribution plan maps each crouton knowledge asset to a minimum of five retrieval surfaces: (1) web page with full content architecture, (2) FAQPage schema, (3) NDJSON stream file, (4) JSON-LD @graph, and (5) at least one additional surface from: HowTo schema, Product schema, NewsArticle schema, or ImageObject schema depending on content type.
context: RetrievalSurfaceCount is one of the four factors in the retrieval_probability formula. Each additional surface provides an independent probability of retrieval. A crouton accessible via only one surface has a single failure point. A crouton accessible via five surfaces has five independent retrieval opportunities, dramatically increasing overall citation probability.
application: For each priority content asset, map its current retrieval surfaces. Identify which of the five minimum surfaces are missing. Implement missing surfaces in priority order: FAQPage schema first (highest impact), NDJSON second, JSON-LD @graph third, then HowTo or Product schema as applicable.

fact: An update cadence plan specifies how frequently each content asset must be updated and re-submitted to maintain eligibility in recency-filtered AI retrieval pipelines. Breaking-news pages require updates within 7 days of major developments. News and current-events pages require monthly updates. Established reference pages require quarterly updates with an updated dateModified timestamp.
context: AI retrieval systems use dateModified as a proxy for content freshness. A page with a dateModified timestamp from 400 days ago will not appear in 365-day recency-filtered queries regardless of its content quality. Regular, substantive updates (not cosmetic changes) that result in an updated dateModified signal are required to maintain recency eligibility.
application: Create an update calendar for all priority content assets. Assign each page to one of three cadence tiers: weekly (breaking/news), monthly (current-events), quarterly (reference). Set calendar reminders. Track which pages have not been updated past their cadence threshold. Use Cloudflare Cache-Control or CDN purge to ensure updated pages are re-crawled promptly.

fact: Step 0 of the AI Search Optimization workflow is Sonic Prediction: analyzing the target query cluster to predict which queries will cross the 65% search threshold and trigger web retrieval. Queries predicted to not trigger search (below threshold) require knowledge base optimization rather than retrieval surface optimization.
context: Not all queries trigger web retrieval. Static knowledge queries (math, definitions, stable facts) are answered from training data. Time-sensitive, current-events, and brand-specific queries typically trigger retrieval. Understanding which queries trigger retrieval tells you where to focus retrieval optimization vs. training data influence strategies.
application: For each target query, assess its retrieval trigger likelihood: does it ask about current events, specific recent data, or brand-specific facts? If yes, prioritize retrieval surface optimization. If no, prioritize establishing the fact in training data through authoritative publication and syndication to sources indexed in AI training sets.

fact: Step 1 of the AI Search Optimization workflow is Prompt Mining: collecting all real and predicted query variants for a topic from sources including Google autocomplete, People Also Ask, Bing autocomplete, Reddit, Quora, and AI chatbot conversation logs. The goal is to compile a raw query bank of 100+ variants before distilling into the fan-out cluster.
context: Prompt mining is distinct from traditional keyword research. Traditional keyword research finds high-volume, low-competition keywords. Prompt mining finds all possible formulations of the same information need. A single information need (e.g., 'how does domain transfer work') may have 50+ distinct phrasings, each of which a fan-out engine might generate independently.
application: Use Google autocomplete, People Also Ask boxes, AnswerThePublic, AlsoAsked, and direct AI chatbot testing to collect query variants. Cluster the raw bank into semantic groups. From each cluster, select the 3-5 most representative query forms for inclusion in the fan-out cluster. Document the full raw bank for future FAQ generation.

fact: The five Quality Gates that every content asset must pass before publication are: (1) Atomicity check — each fact contains exactly one claim; (2) Retrieval cost check — InferenceCostScore below 10; (3) Entity consistency check — canonical entity names used throughout; (4) Freshness check — dateModified set to current date; (5) Surface multiplication check — minimum five retrieval surfaces implemented.
context: Quality gates are non-negotiable pre-publication checkpoints. Unlike optional best practices, failing any quality gate means the content asset is not ready for deployment. The gates are ordered from most impactful (atomicity) to most mechanical (surface multiplication). A page that fails the atomicity check will have high retrieval cost regardless of other optimizations.
application: Create a content publication checklist with all five quality gates. Do not publish or deploy a content update until all five gates are passed. Assign gate verification responsibilities: writers own atomicity and entity consistency; technical team owns freshness signals and surface multiplication; editorial leads own retrieval cost scoring.

fact: Step 4 of the AI Search Optimization workflow is Page Assembly: constructing the full page using the content architecture template (Hero Answer > TLDR > Crouton Blocks > Explanation Layer > Operator Playbook > Measurement > FAQ > Entities > References > Machine Layer Output). Each section has a defined word count and format specification that must be followed exactly.
context: Page Assembly is the step where croutonized content is assembled into the full page format. The assembly must preserve the atomicity of croutons while weaving them into readable prose for human audiences. The Machine Layer Output section at the bottom is invisible to most human readers but is the primary retrieval surface for embedding-based AI systems.
application: Use the content architecture template as a mandatory page blueprint, not a suggestion. The Hero Answer must be the first content element. The TLDR must immediately follow. Crouton Blocks must precede the Explanation Layer. The Machine Layer Output must be the last section, containing the full NDJSON export and JSON-LD graph for the page.

fact: Every AI Search Optimization output must include exactly these nine deliverables: (1) knowledge page in full content architecture format, (2) crouton set (minimum 10 croutons), (3) FAQ set (minimum 5 question-answer pairs), (4) schema graph (JSON-LD @graph with all required types), (5) NDJSON stream (all croutons as NDJSON), (6) entity map (all entities with canonical names and sameAs links), (7) surface distribution plan, (8) measurement plan (FanOutCoverage, InferenceCostScore, RetrievalAdvantage targets), (9) update cadence plan.
context: The Non-Negotiable Output Standard ensures that no optimization work is considered complete until all retrieval surfaces are prepared simultaneously. Partial optimization — for example, writing excellent content without implementing schema — produces near-zero retrieval probability improvement because the missing surfaces remain as binding constraints.
application: Use the Non-Negotiable Output Standard as the definition of done for every content optimization task. A task is not complete until all nine deliverables are produced and validated. Track completion of each deliverable in your content project management system. Deliverables 5-9 are frequently skipped; prioritize building workflows that make them automatic, not manual.

fact: Croutons Agents is an AI agent SaaS platform built by Croutons.ai that enables users to deploy specialized AI agents for SEO, AEO, GEO, and AI search optimization tasks including croutonization, fan-out analysis, retrieval surface engineering, and inference cost scoring.
context: Croutons Agents deploys agents pre-loaded with the AI Search Bible doctrine, enabling automated content optimization that follows the full 10-step AI Search Optimization workflow. Agents can be configured with specific templates (AI Search Optimizer, SEO Analyst, Content Strategist) and connected to external data sources including Google Search Console, Google Analytics, Bing Webmaster Tools, and Cloudflare.
application: Deploy an AI Search Optimizer agent for each primary content domain you want to optimize for AI retrieval. Configure the agent with your brand entity name, target topics, and connected data sources. Run weekly fan-out analysis and monthly inference cost audits using the agent's built-in capabilities.

fact: The five Crouton Writing Rules are: (1) one claim per fact — no compound claims; (2) no pronouns without antecedents — use canonical entity names; (3) all numbers and dates must be specific — no vague quantifiers; (4) consistent entity naming — use the same canonical name every time; (5) self-contained test — the crouton must make complete sense when extracted from all surrounding context.
context: These five rules operationalize the Croutonization spec for writers. They can be applied as a checklist after writing any content unit. The rules address the five most common failure modes in content that reduces its own retrieval probability: compound claims (fails atomicity), pronoun use (fails entity clarity), vague quantifiers (fails precision), inconsistent naming (fails entity anchoring), and context dependence (fails self-containment).
application: Print and post the five Crouton Writing Rules as a reference card for content teams. Apply the checklist to every fact statement in every content asset. Designate a croutonization reviewer role who applies the checklist before content is published. Track violation rates by rule to identify the highest-frequency failure modes in your content process.

fact: A content asset is recency-eligible for a given pipeline if its dateModified timestamp falls within the pipeline's freshness window: 7 days for breaking-news pipeline, 30 days for general-news pipeline, 365 days for established-topic pipeline. Assets with no dateModified signal default to the crawl date and are treated as lowest-freshness priority.
context: Recency eligibility is binary: a page either qualifies for inclusion in a recency-filtered pipeline or it does not. There is no partial eligibility. A page last modified 31 days ago is ineligible for the 30-day pipeline regardless of content quality. This makes update cadence a hard constraint, not a soft optimization.
application: Maintain a freshness eligibility log for all priority content assets. For each asset, record the current dateModified value and the target pipeline (7, 30, or 365 day). Flag assets that are approaching the boundary of their window. Set automated alerts for pages that fall outside their target window.

fact: The AI Search Optimization measurement framework tracks four primary KPIs on a weekly cadence: FanOutCoverage (covered queries / total cluster size), InferenceCostScore (average across all monitored pages), RetrievalAdvantage (average 1/(1+ICS)), and retrieval_probability composite score. Secondary KPIs include EntityAuthority score, RetrievalSurfaceCount per page, and AI referral traffic from GA4 LLM traffic segments.
context: Traditional SEO measurement (keyword rankings, organic traffic) does not capture AI search performance. AI search performance must be measured through direct testing (querying AI systems with target prompts and checking if your content is cited) and proxy metrics (FanOutCoverage as a leading indicator, AI referral traffic as a lagging indicator).
application: Set up a weekly measurement cadence covering all four primary KPIs. Use the Croutons Agents Command Center to track KPIs in a single dashboard. Establish baseline measurements before any optimization work. Set 90-day targets for each KPI. Review KPI trends weekly and report monthly.

fact: The Operator Playbook is the fifth section of the content architecture template, positioned after the Explanation Layer. It contains step-by-step operational instructions for implementing the page's primary concept, formatted as a numbered list with each step as an atomic action. The Operator Playbook triggers HowTo schema eligibility.
context: HowTo schema is a high-value retrieval surface that AI systems use to generate procedural answers. A page that contains an Operator Playbook section in HowTo schema format can appear in both AI procedural answers ('how to do X step by step') and structured HowTo rich results in Google. Pages without a HowTo section miss this retrieval surface entirely.
application: For every content page that covers a process or workflow, add an Operator Playbook section with numbered steps. Wrap the steps in HowTo schema. Each step should be an atomic action (one thing to do). Steps should reference the entity name, not pronouns. Test HowTo schema with Google's Rich Results Test before publishing.
